evidence_text: Model editing allows for precise modifications of learned knowledge, providing controlled updates without requiring full retraining.
retrieval-based approaches explicitly access external information dynamically during inference
Unlike these implicit knowledge expansion methods, which modify the model’s internal parameters
To remain effective, LLMs require updates across multiple dimensions.
Factual knowledge consists of general truths and real-time information
domain knowledge pertains to specialized knowledge
We survey adaptation strategies, including continual learning, model editing, and retrieval-based approaches

Continual Learning (§4) ... Updating Facts (§5.1)
Continual Learning (§4) ... Domain Adaptation (§4.2)
Continual Learning (§4) ... Language Expansion (§4.3)

continual pretraining for updating facts This line of research focuses on updating a language model’s outdated internal factual knowledge by incrementally integrating up-to-date world knowledge
recent works utilize parameter-isolation methods which allocate different parameter subsets to distinct tasks or domains and keep the majority of parameters frozen
DEMix-DAPT (Gururangan et al., 2022) replaces every feed-forward network layer in the Transformer model with a domain expert mixture layer
DEMix-DAPT (Gururangan et al., 2022) replaces every feed-forward network layer in the Transformer model with a domain expert mixture layer, containing one expert per domain
Qin et al. (2022) propose ELLE for efficient lifelong pretraining on various domains
continual learning plugins to the frozen pretrained language models that mitigate catastrophic forgetting
Ke et al. (2023) apply regularization to penalize changes to critical parameters learned from previous data, preventing catastrophic forgetting
Lifelong-MoE (Chen et al., 2023) expands expert capacity progressively
applying continual pretraining to enhance the multilingual capabilities of LLMs
Blevins et al. (2024) extend this approach to the MoE paradigm for better parameter efficiency
Zheng et al. (2024) investigate scaling laws for continual pretraining by training LLMs of varying sizes
Tran (2020), Minixhofer et al. (2022), Dobler and de Melo (2023), Liu et al. (2024b), and Minixhofer et al. (2024) explore advanced tokenization and word embedding techniques
Continual pretraining (CPT) has emerged as a pivotal strategy for adapting LLMs to new languages, or enhancing performance in underrepresented languages without full retraining (Wu et al., 2024b).
Below, we discuss two major areas of expansion enabled by CPT: natural language expansion and programming language expansion.
Below, we discuss two major areas of expansion enabled by CPT: natural language expansion and programming language expansion.
Glot500 (Imani et al., 2023) and EMMA-500 (Ji et al., 2024) enhance multilingual capabilities using CPT
Glot500 (Imani et al., 2023) and EMMA-500 (Ji et al., 2024) enhance multilingual capabilities using CPT
CERT, proposed by Zan et al. (2022), addresses the challenges of library-oriented code generation using unlabeled code corpora.
It employs a two-stage framework to enable LLMs to effectively capture patterns in library-based code snippets.
CodeTask-CL (Yadav et al., 2023) offers a benchmark for continual code learning, encompassing a diverse set of tasks such as code generation, summarization, translation, and refine
Zhang et al. (2023b) propose a non-reinforcement learning approach for continual preference alignment in LLMs.
Their method uses function regularization by computing an optimal policy distribution for each task and applying it to regularize future tasks.
applying it to regularize future tasks, preventing catastrophic forgetting while adapting to new domains.
This provides a single-phase, reinforcement learning-free solution for maintaining alignment across diverse tasks.
Zhang et al. (2024a) introduce Continual Proximal Policy Optimization (CPPO), integrating continual learning.
Yao et al. (2023) and Zhang et al. (2024c) define four key evaluation metrics for model editing.
Yao et al. (2023) and Zhang et al. (2024c) define four key evaluation metrics for model editing.
(1) reliability, ensuring the edited model produces the target prediction for the target input.
(2) generalization, requiring the edited knowledge to apply to all in-scope inputs.
(3) locality, preserving original outputs for unrelated out-of-scope inputs.
(4) portability, extending the generalization metric by assessing how well updated knowledge transfers.
CPPO employs a sample-wise weighting strategy to balance policy learning and knowledge retention
CPPO employs a sample-wise weighting strategy to balance policy learning and knowledge retention
CPPO ... consolidating high-reward behaviors while mitigating overfitting and noise
CPPO ... consolidating high-reward behaviors while mitigating overfitting and noise
CPA is key to keeping LLMs ethical and aligned with evolving user needs
model editing is a subset of knowledge editing that modifies model parameters
model editing is a subset of knowledge editing that modifies model parameters
retrieval-based methods update knowledge dynamically without altering the model’s parameters
model editing research focuses on selectively modifying this knowledge
KnowledgeEditor (De Cao et al., 2021) uses a hypernetwork to predict parameter shifts for modifying a fact
trained via constrained optimization for locality
MEND (Mitchell et al., 2022a) trains a hypernetwork per LLM layer
MEND (Mitchell et al., 2022a) ... decomposes the fine-tuning gradien
Continual learning is a versatile framework for expanding LLM knowledge across facts, domains, languages, and preferences
It excels in large-scale knowledge integration
retaining previously learned knowledge
making it well-suited for tasks like domain adaptation and language expansion
integrating continual learning into the RLHF framework to accommodate evolving human preferences
However, CL has notable limitations, including a lack of precise control compared to model editing (cf. Section 5) and retrieval-based methods (cf. Section 6)
However, CL has notable limitations, including ... inefficiency due to the computational demands of retraining
However, CL has notable limitations, including ... limited applicability in black-box models
Model editing offers a controllable and efficient solution to update factual knowledge and user preferences in LLMs.
Dai et al. (2022) introduce a knowledge attribution method to identify these neurons and directly modify their values via knowledge surgery.
Meng et al. (2022) identify middle-layer feed-forward networks as key to factual predictions and propose ROME
ROME, which updates facts by solving a constrained least-squares problem in the MLP weight matrix.
MEMIT (Meng et al., 2023) extends ROME to modify thousands of facts simultaneously across critical layers while preserving generalization and locality.
BIRD (Ma et al., 2023) introduces bidirectional inverse relationship modeling to mitigate the reverse curse in model editing
Retrieval-based methods have been widely adopted for various domain-specific tasks, e.g., in science and finance.
Retrieval-based methods have been widely adopted for various domain-specific tasks, e.g., in science and finance.
In the biomedical domain, retrieval-based approaches facilitate tasks, such as molecular property identification and drug discovery
In the biomedical domain, retrieval-based approaches facilitate tasks, such as molecular property identification and drug discovery
integrating structured molecular data and information about biomedical entities like proteins, molecules, and diseases
integrating structured molecular data and information about biomedical entities like proteins, molecules, and diseases
integrating structured molecular data and information about biomedical entities like proteins, molecules, and diseases
Wang et al. (2023b) and Li et al. (2024a) introduce retrieval-based frameworks that extract relevant molecular data from databases to guide molecule generation.
Wang et al. (2023b) and Li et al. (2024a) introduce retrieval-based frameworks that extract relevant molecular data from databases to guide molecule generation.
In protein research, retrieval-based approaches enhance protein representation and generation tasks
In protein research, retrieval-based approaches enhance protein representation and generation tasks
An inherent challenge of expanding a model’s knowledge is the emergence of knowledge conflicts
Studies have identified various types of conflicts following knowledge updates, including (i) temporal misalignment
Studies have identified various types of conflicts following knowledge updates, including ... (ii) model inconsistencies

20 years of research from the area of neurosymbolic AI, known as neural-symbolic integration
the combination of deep learning and symbolic reasoning
the combination of deep learning and symbolic reasoning
Pearl proposes a hierarchy consisting of three levels: association, intervention and counterfactual reasoning
Pearl proposes a hierarchy consisting of three levels: association, intervention and counterfactual reasoning
Pearl proposes a hierarchy consisting of three levels: association, intervention and counterfactual reasoning
claims that ML is only capable of achieving association
A neurosymbolic or purely symbolic ML system should be capable of satisfying the requirements of all three of Pearl’s levels
A neurosymbolic or purely symbolic ML system should be capable of satisfying the requirements of all three of Pearl’s levels
e.g. by mapping the neural networks onto symbolic descriptions
good progress is being made towards the extraction of local, measurable counterfactual explanations from black box ML systems
Once a neural network has been endowed with a symbolic interpretation
one has no reason to doubt the ability of a neural system to ask what if questions

the very algorithm for extracting symbolic logic descriptions of the form A → B from trained neural networks uses a form of interrogation of the network akin to the intervention of Bayesian models advocated by Pearl
uses a form of interrogation of the network akin to the intervention of Bayesian models advocated by Pearl
a more important question is representational: which representation is most effective, deep networks or Bayesian networks?
neural networks offer a concrete model of computation
one which can be implemented efficiently by message passing or propagation of activation
and trained by differentiable learning algorithms
the neural information processing community have shown the value of neural computation in practice
which has attracted the curiosity of great minds from symbolic AI
We hope that further collaboration in neurosymbolic AI will help solve many of the issues which are still outstanding
The integration of learning and reasoning through neurosymbolic systems requires a bridge between localist and distributed representations
The integration of learning and reasoning through neurosymbolic systems requires a bridge between localist and distributed representations
The success of deep learning indicates that distributed representations with gradient-based methods are more adequate than localist ones for learning and optimization
both the symbolic and connectionist paradigms share common characteristics
offering benefits when put together in a principled way
offering benefits when put together in a principled way

Change of representation also offers a way of making sense of the value of different neural models and architectures with respect to what is a more formal and better understood area of research: symbolic logic
Neural network-based learning and inference under uncertainty have been expected to address the brittleness and computational complexity of symbolic systems
Symbolism has been expected to provide additional knowledge in the form of constraints for learning
In Kautz’s taxonomy, a Type 1 neural-symbolic integration is standard deep learning
a Type 1 neural-symbolic integration is standard deep learning
Type 2 are hybrid systems such as DeepMind’s AlphaGo and other systems where the core neural network is loosely-coupled with a symbolic problem solver such as Monte Carlo tree search
Type 2 are hybrid systems such as DeepMind’s AlphaGo
a symbolic problem solver such as Monte Carlo tree search
Type 3 is also a hybrid system whereby a neural network focusing on one task interacts via its input and output with a symbolic system specialising in a complementary task
Examples include the neuro-symbolic concept learner and deepProbLog
Examples include the neuro-symbolic concept learner and deepProbLog
In a Type 4 neural-symbolic system, symbolic knowledge is compiled into the training set of a neural network
as well as Logical Neural Networks, where the key concept is to create a 1-to-1 correspondence between neurons and the elements of logical formulas
Type 5 are those tightly-coupled but distributed neural-symbolic systems where a symbolic logic rule is mapped onto an embedding which acts as a soft-constraint on the network’s loss function
Examples of these include Logic Tensor Networks
As put simply by Moshe Vardi “Logic is the Calculus of Computer Science”
Specifically, deep neural networks will require a language for description, as also advocated by Leslie Valiant.
Neural network-based AI ... deals well with large-scale multimodal noisy perceptual data such as text and audio
handles symbol grounding better than symbolic systems since concepts are grounded on feature vectors
and is by definition a computational model
frequently implemented efficiently using propagation of activation and tensor processing units
Symbolic AI is generally localist and discrete
capable of sophisticated reasoning, including temporal, epistemic and nonmonotonic reasoning, planning, extrapolation and reasoning by analogy
Neurosymbolic AI has shown that non-classical logics, in particular many-valued logics, offer an adequate language for describing neural networks
in particular many-valued logics
current recurrent neural networks are capable of computing the logical consequences of propositional modal logic programs and other forms of non-classical reasoning and fragment of first order logic
In the original paper on deep learning [35], a modular system is proposed consisting of a stack of restricted Boltzmann machines (RBMs).
Each RBM learns a joint probability distribution while their symbolic description reflects the result of learning without manipulating probabilities explicitly
The extraction of symbolic descriptions from each RBM is thus made considerably easier [86].
When RBMs are stacked onto a deep belief network, however, the modular extraction of compositional rules may be accompanied by a compounding loss of accuracy
similar to what was proposed with Penalty Logic for Hopfield networks in [59]
an efficient algorithm is presented that extracts propositional rules enriched with confidence values from RBMs, similar to what was proposed with Penalty Logic for Hopfield networks in [59]
systems where unsupervised learning is combined with weakly-supervised classification at distinct levels of abstraction, such as with the use of variational auto-encoders
systems where unsupervised learning is combined with weakly-supervised classification at distinct levels of abstraction, such as with the use of ... generative adversarial networks

We therefore do not advocate the adoption of monoblock networks with millions of parameters. ... loss of modularity seems to be, at least at present from a computational perspective, a price that is too high to pay.
Modularity remains a fundamentally relevant property of any computing system.
Applications of Neurosymbolic AI: One major way of driving advances in AI continues to be through challenging applications, be it language translation
Applications of Neurosymbolic AI: One major way of driving advances in AI continues to be through challenging applications, be it ... computer games
Applications of Neurosymbolic AI: One major way of driving advances in AI continues to be through challenging applications, be it ... protein folding competitions
Language understanding in the broadest sense of the term, including question-answering that requires commonsense reasoning, offers probably the most complete application area for neurosymbolic AI.
Language understanding in the broadest sense of the term, including question-answering that requires commonsense reasoning, offers probably the most complete application area for neurosymbolic AI.
Another very relevant application domain is planning, which requires learning and reasoning over time
Finally, an area where machine learning and knowledge representation and reasoning have complementary strengths is knowledge engineering
Finally, an area where machine learning and knowledge representation and reasoning have complementary strengths is knowledge engineering
including knowledge graphs to be combined with neural networks such as graph neural networks
A common thread across the above examples and applications is the need for modelling cause and effect with the use of implicit information.
This requires learning of general rules and exceptions to the rules that evolve over time.
In such cases, deep learning alone fails when presented with examples from outside the distribution of the training data.
This motivated Judea Pearl’s critique of Machine Learning [55]
In Pearl’s 3-level causal hierarchy (association, intervention and counterfactuals), association involves purely statistical relationships.
In Pearl’s 3-level causal hierarchy (association, intervention and counterfactuals), ... the second level, intervention, ranks higher than association
In Pearl’s 3-level causal hierarchy (association, intervention and counterfactuals), ... the top level invokes counterfactuals
Finally, the top level invokes counterfactuals, a mode of reasoning that reverts to the philosophers David Hume and John Stuart Mill
Finally, the top level invokes counterfactuals, a mode of reasoning that reverts to the philosophers David Hume and John Stuart Mill
neural-symbolic computing can implement all three of Pearl’s levels
Once a symbolic description of the form if A then B has been associated with a neural network, surely the idea of intervention and counterfactual reasoning become possible
Once a symbolic description of the form if A then B has been associated with a neural network, surely the idea of intervention and counterfactual reasoning become possible
Knowledge should be grounded onto vector representations for efficient learning from data based on message passing in neural networks as an efficient computational model

Symbols should become available as a result of querying and knowledge extraction from trained networks, and offer a rich description language at an adequate level of abstraction, enabling infinite uses of finite means, but also compositional discrete reasoning at the symbolic level allowing for extrapolation beyond the data distribution

The combination of learning and reasoning should offer an important alternative to the problem of combinatorial reasoning by learning to reduce the number of effective combinations, thus producing simpler symbolic descriptions as part of the neurosymbolic cycle

CERT, proposed by Zan et al. (2022), addresses the challenges of library-oriented code generation using unlabeled code corpora.
It employs a two-stage framework to enable LLMs to effectively capture patterns in library-based code snippets.
CodeTask-CL (Yadav et al., 2023) offers a benchmark for continual code learning, encompassing a diverse set of tasks such as code generation, summarization, translation, and refine
Zhang et al. (2023b) propose a non-reinforcement learning approach for continual preference alignment in LLMs.
Their method uses function regularization by computing an optimal policy distribution for each task and applying it to regularize future tasks.
applying it to regularize future tasks, preventing catastrophic forgetting while adapting to new domains.
This provides a single-phase, reinforcement learning-free solution for maintaining alignment across diverse tasks.
Zhang et al. (2024a) introduce Continual Proximal Policy Optimization (CPPO), integrating continual learning.
Yao et al. (2023) and Zhang et al. (2024c) define four key evaluation metrics for model editing.
Yao et al. (2023) and Zhang et al. (2024c) define four key evaluation metrics for model editing.
(1) reliability, ensuring the edited model produces the target prediction for the target input.
(2) generalization, requiring the edited knowledge to apply to all in-scope inputs.
(3) locality, preserving original outputs for unrelated out-of-scope inputs.
(4) portability, extending the generalization metric by assessing how well updated knowledge transfers.
CPPO employs a sample-wise weighting strategy to balance policy learning and knowledge retention
CPPO employs a sample-wise weighting strategy to balance policy learning and knowledge retention
CPPO ... consolidating high-reward behaviors while mitigating overfitting and noise

model editing research focuses on selectively modifying this knowledge

trained via constrained optimization for locality

It excels in large-scale knowledge integration
retaining previously learned knowledge
making it well-suited for tasks like domain adaptation and language expansion
integrating continual learning into the RLHF framework to accommodate evolving human preferences

Model editing offers a controllable and efficient solution to update factual knowledge and user preferences in LLMs.
Dai et al. (2022) introduce a knowledge attribution method to identify these neurons and directly modify their values via knowledge surgery.
Meng et al. (2022) identify middle-layer feed-forward networks as key to factual predictions and propose ROME
ROME, which updates facts by solving a constrained least-squares problem in the MLP weight matrix.
MEMIT (Meng et al., 2023) extends ROME to modify thousands of facts simultaneously across critical layers while preserving generalization and locality.
BIRD (Ma et al., 2023) introduces bidirectional inverse relationship modeling to mitigate the reverse curse in model editing

the combination of deep learning and symbolic reasoning

claims that ML is only capable of achieving association

one has no reason to doubt the ability of a neural system to ask what if questions

the very algorithm for extracting symbolic logic descriptions of the form A → B from trained neural networks uses a form of interrogation of the network akin to the intervention of Bayesian models advocated by Pearl
uses a form of interrogation of the network akin to the intervention of Bayesian models advocated by Pearl
a more important question is representational: which representation is most effective, deep networks or Bayesian networks?
neural networks offer a concrete model of computation
one which can be implemented efficiently by message passing or propagation of activation
and trained by differentiable learning algorithms
the neural information processing community have shown the value of neural computation in practice
which has attracted the curiosity of great minds from symbolic AI
We hope that further collaboration in neurosymbolic AI will help solve many of the issues which are still outstanding
The integration of learning and reasoning through neurosymbolic systems requires a bridge between localist and distributed representations
The integration of learning and reasoning through neurosymbolic systems requires a bridge between localist and distributed representations
The success of deep learning indicates that distributed representations with gradient-based methods are more adequate than localist ones for learning and optimization
both the symbolic and connectionist paradigms share common characteristics
offering benefits when put together in a principled way
offering benefits when put together in a principled way

Change of representation also offers a way of making sense of the value of different neural models and architectures with respect to what is a more formal and better understood area of research: symbolic logic
Neural network-based learning and inference under uncertainty have been expected to address the brittleness and computational complexity of symbolic systems
Symbolism has been expected to provide additional knowledge in the form of constraints for learning
In Kautz’s taxonomy, a Type 1 neural-symbolic integration is standard deep learning
a Type 1 neural-symbolic integration is standard deep learning
Type 2 are hybrid systems such as DeepMind’s AlphaGo and other systems where the core neural network is loosely-coupled with a symbolic problem solver such as Monte Carlo tree search
Type 2 are hybrid systems such as DeepMind’s AlphaGo
a symbolic problem solver such as Monte Carlo tree search
Type 3 is also a hybrid system whereby a neural network focusing on one task interacts via its input and output with a symbolic system specialising in a complementary task
Examples include the neuro-symbolic concept learner and deepProbLog
Examples include the neuro-symbolic concept learner and deepProbLog
In a Type 4 neural-symbolic system, symbolic knowledge is compiled into the training set of a neural network
as well as Logical Neural Networks, where the key concept is to create a 1-to-1 correspondence between neurons and the elements of logical formulas
Type 5 are those tightly-coupled but distributed neural-symbolic systems where a symbolic logic rule is mapped onto an embedding which acts as a soft-constraint on the network’s loss function
Examples of these include Logic Tensor Networks
As put simply by Moshe Vardi “Logic is the Calculus of Computer Science”
Specifically, deep neural networks will require a language for description, as also advocated by Leslie Valiant.
Neural network-based AI ... deals well with large-scale multimodal noisy perceptual data such as text and audio
handles symbol grounding better than symbolic systems since concepts are grounded on feature vectors
and is by definition a computational model
frequently implemented efficiently using propagation of activation and tensor processing units
Symbolic AI is generally localist and discrete
capable of sophisticated reasoning, including temporal, epistemic and nonmonotonic reasoning, planning, extrapolation and reasoning by analogy
Neurosymbolic AI has shown that non-classical logics, in particular many-valued logics, offer an adequate language for describing neural networks
in particular many-valued logics
current recurrent neural networks are capable of computing the logical consequences of propositional modal logic programs and other forms of non-classical reasoning and fragment of first order logic
In the original paper on deep learning [35], a modular system is proposed consisting of a stack of restricted Boltzmann machines (RBMs).
Each RBM learns a joint probability distribution while their symbolic description reflects the result of learning without manipulating probabilities explicitly
The extraction of symbolic descriptions from each RBM is thus made considerably easier [86].
When RBMs are stacked onto a deep belief network, however, the modular extraction of compositional rules may be accompanied by a compounding loss of accuracy
similar to what was proposed with Penalty Logic for Hopfield networks in [59]
an efficient algorithm is presented that extracts propositional rules enriched with confidence values from RBMs, similar to what was proposed with Penalty Logic for Hopfield networks in [59]
systems where unsupervised learning is combined with weakly-supervised classification at distinct levels of abstraction, such as with the use of variational auto-encoders
systems where unsupervised learning is combined with weakly-supervised classification at distinct levels of abstraction, such as with the use of ... generative adversarial networks

We therefore do not advocate the adoption of monoblock networks with millions of parameters. ... loss of modularity seems to be, at least at present from a computational perspective, a price that is too high to pay.
Modularity remains a fundamentally relevant property of any computing system.

Language understanding in the broadest sense of the term, including question-answering that requires commonsense reasoning, offers probably the most complete application area for neurosymbolic AI.
Language understanding in the broadest sense of the term, including question-answering that requires commonsense reasoning, offers probably the most complete application area for neurosymbolic AI.
Another very relevant application domain is planning, which requires learning and reasoning over time
Finally, an area where machine learning and knowledge representation and reasoning have complementary strengths is knowledge engineering
Finally, an area where machine learning and knowledge representation and reasoning have complementary strengths is knowledge engineering
including knowledge graphs to be combined with neural networks such as graph neural networks
A common thread across the above examples and applications is the need for modelling cause and effect with the use of implicit information.
This requires learning of general rules and exceptions to the rules that evolve over time.
In such cases, deep learning alone fails when presented with examples from outside the distribution of the training data.
This motivated Judea Pearl’s critique of Machine Learning [55]
In Pearl’s 3-level causal hierarchy (association, intervention and counterfactuals), association involves purely statistical relationships.
In Pearl’s 3-level causal hierarchy (association, intervention and counterfactuals), ... the second level, intervention, ranks higher than association
In Pearl’s 3-level causal hierarchy (association, intervention and counterfactuals), ... the top level invokes counterfactuals
Finally, the top level invokes counterfactuals, a mode of reasoning that reverts to the philosophers David Hume and John Stuart Mill
Finally, the top level invokes counterfactuals, a mode of reasoning that reverts to the philosophers David Hume and John Stuart Mill
neural-symbolic computing can implement all three of Pearl’s levels

Knowledge should be grounded onto vector representations for efficient learning from data based on message passing in neural networks as an efficient computational model

Symbols should become available as a result of querying and knowledge extraction from trained networks, and offer a rich description language at an adequate level of abstraction, enabling infinite uses of finite means, but also compositional discrete reasoning at the symbolic level allowing for extrapolation beyond the data distribution

The combination of learning and reasoning should offer an important alternative to the problem of combinatorial reasoning by learning to reduce the number of effective combinations, thus producing simpler symbolic descriptions as part of the neurosymbolic cycle
As an example, consider a Variational Autoencoder which learns in unsupervised fashion to maximise mutual information between pixel inputs and a latent code or some other embedding
The Third AI Summer, Henry Kautz’s AAAI 2020 Robert S. Engelmore Memorial Award Lecture, which introduced a taxonomy for neurosymbolic computing;
The Director of MIT/IBM Watson AI laboratory David Cox IAAI keynote address
IAAI keynote address, which focused on neurosymbolic AI and applications on vision and language understanding, machine commonsense, question answering, argumentation and XAI.
At the Turing award session and fireside conversation with Daniel Kahneman
Kahneman made his point clear by stating that on top of deep learning, a System 2 symbolic layer is needed.
He argued that S1 (as the intuitive parallel system) is capable of understanding language
in contradiction with Yoshua Bengio’s account of deep learning’s S1 and S2.
Kahneman also stated that S2 (as the sequential deliberative system) is most probably performing symbol manipulation as argued by Gary Marcus
In his “next decade of AI” paper, Marcus argues strongly in favour of hybrid systems
Stephen Muggleton noted at another debate on the future of AI, his goal is to shorten the wait for the next Newton or Einstein, or Alan Turing.
Muggleton’s bet is on the use of higher-order logic representations and meta-interpretive learning.
Muggleton’s bet is on the use of higher-order logic representations and meta-interpretive learning.
With the neural-symbolic methodology, the goal is to develop neural network models with a symbolic interpretation.
The key is how to learn representations neurally and make them available for use symbolically (as for example when an AI system is asked to explain itself).
In this paper, we have argued for modularity as an important ingredient, allowing one to refer to large parts of the network by the composition of symbols and relations among them.
We have argued for the use of first-order logic as this language, as a canonical form of representation...
but also other forms of non-classical representation such as nonmonotonic and modal logic and logic programming.
Once a complex network can be described symbolically, ideally in an abstract compact form as in e = mc2 , any style of deductive reasoning becomes possible.
Reasoning is obviously another fundamental ingredient, either within or outside the network, exact or approximate.
Finally, symbolic meaning can serve to improve performance of S1.
symbols which have been learned, derived or even invented can act as constraints on the large network and help improve learning performance as part of a positive cycle of learning and reasoning.
Constraint satisfaction as part of the interplay between learning and reasoning is therefore another ingredient.
With the above five ingredients of neurosymbolic AI - gradient-based optimization, modularity, symbolic language, reasoning and constraint satisfaction
